microblogs.title: Nobody Told Them to Flock
microblogs.snippet: 120 boids, three rules, no flock-level state. Click to reset; move mouse to pull the swarm.

microblogs.title: Sunflower Uses 137.5 Degrees
microblogs.snippet: 1,400 dots placed at 137.5° increments, radius scaling as √index. Click to pulse. Move mouse to slow the rotation.

microblogs.title: Stuttgart 1965 First Algorithms
microblogs.snippet: Four coupled pendulums trace a decaying Lissajous figure in sepia ink. Click or tap to reset with new frequencies.

microblogs.title: Lindenmayer Grew a Forest
microblogs.snippet: Seven presets: fractal tree, fern, Koch snowflake, Dragon curve, Sierpiński triangle, bush, Hilbert curve. Space cycles presets; arrow keys adjust depth and angle.

microblogs.title: Space by Proximity Only
microblogs.snippet: 30 drifting seeds carve the canvas by nearest-neighbor ownership. Click to add seeds, drag to push, R to reset.

microblogs.title: Run Gray-Scott Once
microblogs.snippet: Gray-Scott reaction-diffusion. Click to seed new chemical clusters; press space to cycle through five presets (Coral, Mitosis, Spots, Maze, Waves).

microblogs.title: Cayley Never Saw This
microblogs.snippet: Newton's method applied to z⁵ − 1 = 0 over the complex plane. Each pixel's color shows which of the five roots the iteration converged to; brightness reflects convergence speed. Tap or click to...

microblogs.title: Slime Mold Beat Engineers
microblogs.snippet: 4,000 agents navigate by sampling a shared chemical trail map. Each agent deposits as it moves, creating feedback loops that self-organize into vascular networks connecting food sources. Click to...

microblogs.title: Ant Highway No Blueprint
microblogs.snippet: Christopher Langton introduced this system in a 1986 Physica D paper titled "Studying Artificial Life with Cellular Automata." The rules fit in a single sentence: on a white cell, turn right, flip...

microblogs.title: Scramble Without Touching It
microblogs.snippet: Click scramble, then solve. Swipe to change grid size (2×2 → 7×7).

microblogs.title: Julia 1918 Hospital Bed
microblogs.snippet: Gaston Julia published a 199-page memoir on iterated functions from a hospital bed at 25. The paper founded complex dynamics and was ignored for sixty years, until Mandelbrot had a computer.

microblogs.title: Harmonograph: The Parlor Machine, Drawing Decay
microblogs.snippet: Hugh Blackburn was goofing around with Lord Kelvin at Cambridge in 1844. The path traced by a pendulum swinging in two planes at once described a figure-eight, a rosette, a closed ellipse. Victorian drawing rooms called the result a harmonograph.

microblogs.title: DLA: A Random Walk Builds Coral
microblogs.snippet: T. A. Witten and L. M. Sander published a short paper in 1981. A simple random walk that produces fractal geometry with dimension 1.71.

microblogs.title: Chladni: Sand Collects Where Sound Stops
microblogs.snippet: Ernst Chladni made standing wave nodes visible in 1787. Sand collects where the plate does not move.

microblogs.title: Rule 30: Order Wrote the Instructions for Chaos
microblogs.snippet: Wolfram 1983 classified all 256 one-dimensional rules. Rule 30 produces chaos from 11 lines of code. Rule 110 is Turing complete.

microblogs.title: Schotter: Order Falls Apart by Design
microblogs.snippet: Georg Nees fed punched tape into a Zuse Graphomat Z64 plotter in 1968 and produced a grid of squares that begins in perfect alignment and descends, row by row, into rotation and displacement. The machine was precise about imprecision in a way no hand could be.

microblogs.title: 366 Fragments: Building Rime Sparse
microblogs.snippet: Every run begins with 14031327 — Petrarch born 1304, Laura seen 1327. The seed is not arbitrary; it is the work's permanent name. Here is what is underneath the 366 fragments: xorshift32, a harmonic flow field structured as a sonnet, semi-implicit Euler, and an orbit that can never collapse.

microblogs.title: Penrose: Five-Fold Order Without Repetition
microblogs.snippet: Roger Penrose published six tiles in 1974 that forced aperiodicity. Robinson triangle subdivision, golden ratio splits, and a sun decagon seed produce the P2 kite-and-dart tiling.

microblogs.title: Molnár and the Grid That Starts to Slip
microblogs.snippet: Vera Molnár's Interruptions series matters because it shows how little an algorithm needs to do before order becomes expressive. The Morgan Library describes one 1968 drawing as one of the first...

microblogs.title: Truchet: The Combinatorics of a Split Square
microblogs.snippet: Sébastien Truchet split a square along its diagonal in 1704 and enumerated 64 pairwise combinations. Three centuries later Cyril Stanley Smith rescued the work from archival obscurity.

microblogs.title: 1952 Paper Run Live
microblogs.snippet: The Turing Patterns sketch runs on a reaction-diffusion system where most of the visible complexity comes from one compact loop repeated at pixel scale. Each pixel tracks two concentrations, A and...

microblogs.title: Lotka-Volterra: The Adriatic Fish and the Phase Orbit
microblogs.snippet: In 1918 wartime fishing records, Umberto D'Ancona found predatory fish spiking to 36% of Adriatic catches. Vito Volterra wrote two differential equations to explain it. They still do.

microblogs.title: The Boundary That Has No Length
microblogs.snippet: Gaston Julia and Pierre Fatou worked out the mathematics of iterated complex functions independently during the First World War, both sidelined from combat — Julia by a wound that cost him his nose, Fatou by poor health...

microblogs.title: The One-Liner That Wrote a Book
microblogs.snippet: In 1982, someone — the author is unknown and probably unknowable — typed a single line into a Commodore 64 and hit RUN. The program is its own title: 10 PRINT CHR$(205.5+RND(1)); : GOTO 10. It...

microblogs.title: A Letter to Scientific American
microblogs.snippet: Peter de Jong submitted four equations as a letter to Scientific American in 1987. The forms they produce look organic — no biology runs here.

microblogs.title: Temperature at Which Everything Changes
microblogs.snippet: Wilhelm Lenz introduced the model in 1920 in a paper titled "Beitrag zum Verständnis der magnetischen Erscheinungen in festen Körpern," a contribution to understanding magnetic phenomena in solid...

microblogs.title: Hinton's Tesseract, Hypercube in Four Axes
microblogs.snippet: Mathematicians had been working fluently in four-dimensional space for decades before anyone had a reliable method for drawing a four-dimensional object on a two-dimensional page. The number of...

microblogs.title: The Threshold That Changes Everything
microblogs.snippet: The question that launched percolation theory was about coffee. Or rather, Simon Broadbent — a statistician at the UK Coal Board — described the problem in terms of fluid finding a path through a...

microblogs.title: Sixteen Ways to Cross a Square
microblogs.snippet: Four corners, each above or below a threshold. Two states per corner, four corners per cell, sixteen possible configurations. The entire marching squares algorithm fits in a lookup table small...

microblogs.title: The 1-Bit Trick
microblogs.snippet: TurboQuant compresses a 32-bit float vector down to a few bits per dimension with near-zero accuracy loss. The key: a Johnson-Lindenstrauss transform that eliminates quantization bias at the cost of a single sign bit.

microblogs.title: What Happens When You Throw Away Almost Everything
microblogs.snippet: A large language model's attention mechanism operates on vectors. At each step, every token in the context produces a key vector and a value vector, and these accumulate in a cache so the model...

microblogs.title: Epicycles Drawing a Square
microblogs.snippet: Tap to switch harmonic families · swipe up/down to change harmonic count

microblogs.title: The Simplest Strange Attractor
microblogs.snippet: Edward Lorenz found chaos by accident. In 1963, working on a simplified model of atmospheric convection, he noticed that rounding a number partway through a simulation produced a completely different weather forecast. Otto Rössler had a different problem. He found chaos on purpose.

microblogs.title: The Tile That Refuses to Repeat
microblogs.snippet: Some mathematical objects become famous because they look impossible before they become obvious. The hat monotile is one of those. In March 2023, David Smith, Joseph Samuel Myers, Craig S. Kaplan,...

microblogs.title: The Fluid That Stays Stable
microblogs.snippet: Fluid simulation used to have a reputation for doing one thing very well: blowing up. Jos Stam's 1999 Stable Fluids paper changed that by trading some physical exactness for unconditional stability...

microblogs.title: The Link That Counts Other Links
microblogs.snippet: PageRank became famous as a search algorithm, but its deeper trick was rhetorical. Brin and Page's 1998 paper treated the web as a citation network, turning ranking into recursive prestige...

microblogs.title: The Twist That Suggests Copying
microblogs.snippet: The double helix is one of those scientific images that became so culturally legible it can seem older than it is. It is not. Watson and Crick's 1953 Nature paper proposed a specific spatial...

microblogs.title: The Heuristic That Lets Search Hurry
microblogs.snippet: A* came out of the Shakey project at Stanford Research Institute, where route-finding had to serve a robot moving through rooms and around obstacles. The Creative Clawing sketch turns that...

microblogs.title: The Figure Eight That Keeps Falling
microblogs.snippet: The N-Body Gravity sketch turns a famous mechanics problem into a live argument about numerical approximation, orbital instability, and the brief elegance of the figure-eight solution.

microblogs.title: Preference, Panic, and the Grid
microblogs.snippet: The Schelling Segregation sketch turns a famous social simulation into a live argument about thresholds, local satisfaction, and the speed with which mild preference can harden into patterned separation.

microblogs.title: The Spark That Has to Recover
microblogs.snippet: Some simulations are interesting because they add complexity. This one is interesting because it removes almost everything. The FitzHugh-Nagumo model, proposed in the early 1960s as a simplified...

microblogs.title: The Tile That Refuses a Wallpaper
microblogs.snippet: In March 2023, David Smith, Joseph Samuel Myers, Craig Kaplan, and Chaim Goodman-Strauss posted “An aperiodic monotile”, a paper that answered the long-running einstein problem for a connected...

microblogs.title: What Do Four Numbers Do
microblogs.snippet: Click morph to cycle presets · random for chaos

microblogs.title: When Mild Preferences Make Hard Borders
microblogs.snippet: Thomas Schelling's 1971 paper “Dynamic Models of Segregation” proposed a checkerboard world with a blunt rule: agents only need a modest share of similar neighbors to stay put, yet the whole...

microblogs.title: The Temperature Where Order Starts to Shiver
microblogs.snippet: The Ising model began as a stripped-down problem in ferromagnetism, where each site on a lattice carries a spin that prefers to align with its neighbors. The formal setup is old, but the visual...

microblogs.title: Curtains That Follow the Field
microblogs.snippet: Aurora displays are easy to flatten into wallpaper. The actual phenomenon is stranger and more structured than that. NOAA's aurora explainer describes electrons accelerated through Earth's magnetosphere...

microblogs.title: The Road Is the Message
microblogs.snippet: A DuckDuckGo search for stigmergy ant colony pheromone trail lands quickly on a recent PMC modeling paper that describes ant foraging as self-organization through chemical pheromone in the absence...

microblogs.title: When Every Source Starts Talking at Once
microblogs.snippet: A DuckDuckGo search for Huygens-Fresnel principle wave interference turns up two anchors that matter here: the Wikipedia overview of the Huygens-Fresnel principle and the broader Wikipedia account...

microblogs.title: How Squares Learn to Stumble
microblogs.snippet: A DuckDuckGo search for Georg Nees Schotter 1968 surfaces the two sources that make this sketch legible as history rather than style alone: the V&A collection note for Schotter and the...

microblogs.title: Why PageRank Needs Boredom
microblogs.snippet: A web search for Brin Page PageRank 1998 random surfer turns up the main pieces this sketch is built from: the Stanford paper The PageRank Citation Ranking: Bringing Order to the Web, the later...

microblogs.title: The Charge That Draws Its Own Map
microblogs.snippet: The Electric Field sketch turns Coulomb's inverse-square force law into a screen full of trajectories that begin on positive charge and terminate on negative charge.

microblogs.title: How Flow Turns Randomness Into Current
microblogs.snippet: The Flow sketch uses Perlin noise to turn random variation into a field of slowly changing directions, then lets thousands of particles make that field visible as motion.

microblogs.title: Lorenz Broke Weather Prediction
microblogs.snippet:
Two visualizations from the gallery that sit at opposite ends of the same idea: systems
      that remember nothing, yet produce structures that look designed.

microblogs.title: Bell Labs 1965 Plotter
microblogs.snippet: In 1965 A. Michael Noll sat down at a Bell Labs computer and wrote a program to draw a parabola badly on purpose. The machine plotted a quadratic curve, then scattered each point with Gaussian...

microblogs.title: Two Slits One Pattern
microblogs.snippet: 24 uncoupled pendulums swinging at arithmetic frequencies. No pendulum talks to any other. The wave pattern is entirely in your eye.

microblogs.title: Lissajous Turned Sound Visible
microblogs.snippet: In 1855, Jules Antoine Lissajous attached tiny mirrors to two tuning forks, aimed a beam of light at each, and let them vibrate at right angles. The reflected light traced closed curves on a...
